solution: The antiderivative is \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{3}x^{3} + \dfrac{[[q]]}{2}x^{2} + [[r]]x \); evaluating from \( [[a]] \) to \( [[b]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the integrand at \( x=[[b]] \) instead of computing the integral.
This uses a right-endpoint rectangle (integrand at \( [[b]] \) times the width) instead of the exact integral.
This uses a left-endpoint rectangle (integrand at \( [[a]] \) times the width) instead of the exact integral.
This mistakenly substitutes \( [[b]]-[[a]] \) for \( x \) in the antiderivative instead of evaluating at each endpoint separately.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[m]]x + ([[k]]) \). What is the NET (signed) area between the graph of \( f \) and the \( x \)-axis on \( \left[ [[a]], [[b]] \right] \), i.e. \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx \)?
solution: The net signed area equals the definite integral itself (regions below the axis contribute negatively): \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left([[m]]x+([[k]])\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses a right-endpoint rectangle approximation instead of the exact integral.
This uses a left-endpoint rectangle approximation instead of the exact integral.
This wrongly assumes the answer must be positive, taking the absolute value of the true net (signed) area.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[k]] \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( [[k]] \) from the correct value in the other direction.
This forgets to multiply by the \( 2/3 \) coefficient from the power rule.
This uses \( \sqrt{x} \) itself as its own antiderivative instead of \( \tfrac{2}{3}x^{3/2} \).
This treats the integrand as if it were the constant \( [[c]]/2 \) rather than \( [[c]]\sqrt{x} \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

stem: The right-endpoint Riemann sum for \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[c]]x\,dx \) using \( n \) equal subintervals is \( R_n = \dfrac{[[c]]\,[[b]]^{2}(n+1)}{2n} \). What is \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} R_n \) (i.e., the value of the definite integral)?
solution: As \( n\to\infty \), \( \dfrac{n+1}{n}\to 1 \), so \( R_n \to \dfrac{[[c]]\,[[b]]^{2}}{2} = [[answer]] \), which matches \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[c]]x\,dx \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( 1/2 \), forgetting the \( 2n \) in the denominator of \( R_n \).
This wrongly assumes the whole expression vanishes as \( n\to\infty \).
This incorrectly splits \( (n+1)/n \) into a limit of \( 1 \) plus a leftover additive \( [[c]] \) term.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.

stem: The right-endpoint Riemann sum for \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[c]]x^{2}\,dx \) using \( n \) equal subintervals is \( R_n = \dfrac{[[c]]\,[[b]]^{3}(n+1)(2n+1)}{6n^{2}} \). What is \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} R_n \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong limiting coefficient (\( 1/2 \) instead of \( 1/3 \)) for the leading term.
This drops the \( 1/3 \) factor entirely.
This wrongly assumes the whole expression vanishes as \( n\to\infty \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.
